name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
۞ مَا نَنسَخْ مِنْ ءَايَةٍ أَوْ نُنسِهَا نَأْتِ بِخَيْرٍ مِّنْهَآ أَوْ مِثْلِهَآ ۗ أَلَمْ تَعْلَمْ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (١٠٦)
```

```english
For any verse that We abrogate or cause to be forgotten, We bring another which is better than it, or similar to it. Do you not know that Allah has power over all things? (106)
```

```arabic
أَلَمْ تَعْلَمْ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَهُۥ مُلْكُ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ وَمَا لَكُم مِّن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مِن وَلِىٍّ وَلَا نَصِيرٍ (١٠٧)
```

```english
Do you not know that to Allah belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth? And besides Allah, you do not have any guardian or helper. (107)
```

## (Commentary)

The two verses concern abrogation (النسخ). It is well known that abrogation in the sense recognized by the jurists (الفقهاء)—which is the disclosure of the end of a ruling’s duration and the expiration of its term—is a technical term derived from the verse and based upon it. It is among the instances of what is derived from the verse regarding the meaning of abrogation, according to the apparent generality (إطلاق) of the verse.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **مَا نَنسَخْ** (Whatever ayah We abrogate). Abrogation (النسخ) is removal (الإزالة). It is said: "The sun abrogated (نسخت) the shadow" when it removes it and takes it away. The Exalted says: **وَمَا أَرْسَلْنَا مِن قَبْلِكَ مِن رَّسُولٍ وَلَا نَبِيٍّ إِلَّا إِذَا تَمَنَّىٰ أَلْقَى الشَّيْطَانُ فِي أُمْنِيَّتِهِ فَيَنسَخُ اللَّهُ مَا يُلْقِي الشَّيْطَانُ** **We did not send before you any apostle or prophet but that when he recited [the [divine] messages] Satan cast [doubts] into his recitation. Thereupon Allah abrogates what Satan casts** (22:52). Also from this is their saying: "I abrogated (نسخت) the book" when it is transcribed from one copy to another; it is as if the book has been taken away and replaced in its stead. For this reason, the word "abrogation" (النسخ) was substituted for "replacement" (التبديل) in His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِذَا بَدَّلْنَا آيَةً مَّكَانَ آيَةٍ وَاللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا يُنَزِّلُ قَالُوا إِنَّمَا أَنتَ مُفْتَرٍ بَلْ أَكْثَرُهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ** **When We replace a verse with another—and Allah knows best what He sends down—they say, ‘You are indeed a fabricator.’ Rather, most of them do not know** (16:101). In any case, abrogation does not necessitate the removal of the verse itself from existence or the nullification of its occurrence. Rather, since the ruling was attached to the description—which is the "verse" (الآية) or "sign" (العلامة)—along with the reasoning (التعليل) that follows it in the verse in His saying, the Exalted: "Do you not know...", etc., this indicates that what is meant by abrogation is the removal of the effect of the verse insofar as it is a verse. I mean the removal of a thing's status as a verse (آية) and a sign (علامة) while preserving its essence (أصل). Thus, through abrogation, its effect—whether a legal obligation (تكليف) or otherwise—is removed while its essence remains. This is what is understood from the pairing of His saying: **نُنسِهَا** (We cause it to be forgotten) with His saying: **مَا نَنسَخْ** (Whatever We abrogate). Causing to forget (الإنساء) is the causative form of forgetting (النسيان), which is removal from knowledge (العلم), just as abrogation (النسخ) is removal from external reality (العين). Thus, the meaning is: whatever verse We remove from external reality or from knowledge, We bring one better than it or like it.

Furthermore, a thing's status as a verse (آية) varies according to different things, aspects, and perspectives. Some parts of the Quran are a verse of Allah, the Glorified, by virtue of the inability of humans to produce the like of it. Divine rulings and obligations (التكاليف) are verses of Him, the Exalted, by virtue of the attainment of God-consciousness (التقوى) and proximity (القرب) to Him through them. External beings (الموجودات العينية) are verses of Him, the Exalted, by virtue of their existence disclosing the existence of their Maker, and by the particularities of their existence disclosing the particularities of His attributes and names, the Glorified. The prophets of Allah and His friends (الأولياء) are verses of Him, the Exalted, by virtue of their calling to Him through word and deed, and so on. For this reason, a verse accepts intensity and weakness; Allah, the Exalted, said: **لَقَدْ رَأَىٰ مِنْ آيَاتِ رَبِّهِ الْكُبْرَىٰ** **He certainly saw some of the greatest signs of his Lord** (53:18).

From another perspective, a verse might be a verse from a single aspect, or it might possess many aspects. Its abrogation and removal can be conceived in its single aspect, such as its destruction (إهلاك), just as it can be conceived in some of its aspects to the exclusion of others if it possesses many aspects—such as a verse of the Quran being abrogated in terms of its legal ruling (حكم شرعي) while remaining in terms of its eloquence (بلاغة) and inimitability (إعجاز), and the like.

This meaning that we have inferred from the generality of the meaning of abrogation is what is supported by the general reasoning (التعليل) understood from His saying, the Exalted: **أَلَمْ تَعْلَمْ أَنَّ اللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ * أَلَمْ تَعْلَمْ أَنَّ اللَّهَ لَهُ مُلْكُ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ** **Do you not know that Allah has power over all things? Do you not know that to Allah belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth?** (2:106-107). This is because the imagined objection in this context, or the actual objection from the Jews as reported regarding the occasion of revelation (شأن النزول) of the verse concerning the meaning of abrogation, relates to it from two perspectives:

**First:** From the perspective that if the verse is from Allah, the Exalted, it must preserve a real interest (مصلحة حقيقية) that nothing else can preserve. If the verse were removed, that interest would be lost, and nothing could take its place to preserve that interest or compensate for the lost benefit of creation and the interest of the servants. His state, the Exalted, is not like the state of His servants, nor is His knowledge like their knowledge, which changes with the change of external factors—such that His knowledge relates to an interest one day and He decrees a ruling, then His knowledge changes tomorrow and relates to another interest that was missed yesterday, so the ruling changes, and He decrees the nullification of what He previously ruled and the arrival of another later. Thus, a new ruling would emerge every day, and one color would appear after another, as is the case with servants who do not encompass the aspects of benefit in things; their rulings and conditions change with the change of knowledge regarding interests and harms, in terms of increase, decrease, origination, and persistence. This perspective traces back to the denial of the generality and absolute nature of Power (القدرة).

**Second:** That even if Power is absolute, the realization of creation (الإيجاد) and the actuality of existence (فعلية الوجود) make change impossible. For a thing does not change from what it has necessarily become. This is like a human being in his voluntary act (الفعل الاختياري); the act is voluntary for the human as long as it has not issued from him, but once it has issued, its establishment becomes necessary (ضروري) and is no longer voluntary for him. This perspective traces back to the denial of the absolute nature of Ownership (الملكية) and the impermissibility of certain disposals after the reins have been released by others, as the Jews said: "The hand of Allah is tied." Thus, the Glorified pointed to the answer to the first by His saying: **أَلَمْ تَعْلَمْ أَنَّ اللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ** (Do you not know that Allah has power over all things?), meaning He is not unable to establish what is better than what was lost or to establish what is like what was lost in its place. He pointed to the answer to the second by His saying: **أَلَمْ تَعْلَمْ أَنَّ اللَّهَ لَهُ مُلْكُ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَمَا لَكُم مِّن دُونِ اللَّهِ مِن وَلِيٍّ وَلَا نَصِيرٍ** (Do you not know that to Allah belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth? And besides Allah you do not have any guardian or helper), meaning that the kingdom of the heavens and the earth belongs to Allah, the Glorified, so He may dispose of His kingdom however He wills. No one else possesses any part of the kingdom such that it would necessitate the closing of one of the doors of His disposal, the Glorified, or be a barrier against any of His disposals. Nothing owns anything, neither originally nor by His granting of ownership (تمليك). For the granting of ownership that He grants to others is not like our granting of ownership to one another in a way that nullifies the ownership of the first and establishes the ownership of the second; rather, He is the Owner (مالك) of what He grants to others at the very moment they own it. If we look at the reality of the matter, absolute ownership and absolute disposal belong to Him alone. If we look at what He has made us own through His ownership without independence (استقلال), He is the Guardian (الولي) for us. And if we look at the apparent independence He has bestowed upon us—which is in reality poverty in the form of wealth, and dependency in the form of independence—it is also not possible for us to manage our affairs without His assistance and help; thus, He is the Helper (النصير) for us.

This which we have mentioned is what is necessitated by the apparent restriction (الحصر) in His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَهُ مُلْكُ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ** (Indeed, to Allah belongs the kingdom of the heavens and the earth). Thus, His saying, the Exalted: **أَلَمْ تَعْلَمْ أَنَّ اللَّهَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ * أَلَمْ تَعْلَمْ أَنَّ اللَّهَ لَهُ مُلْكُ السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ** is arranged according to the order of the two imagined objections. A proof that they are two distinct objections is the separation between the two sentences without a conjunction. His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَا لَكُم مِّن دُونِ اللَّهِ مِن وَلِيٍّ وَلَا نَصِيرٍ** (And besides Allah you do not have any guardian or helper) contains two matters that are like completions of the answer. That is: even if you do not look at His absolute ownership, but rather look at the bestowed ownership you possess, since it is a bestowed ownership without separation or independence, He alone is your Guardian (ولي); thus, He may dispose of you and what you possess however He wills. And if you do not look at your lack of independence in ownership, but rather look at the appearance of the ownership and independence you possess and freeze upon that alone, you will see that the power, ownership, and independence you have do not suffice on their own, nor do they make your goals obedient to you or subject to your intentions and will alone; rather, there must be Allah's assistance and help with them. Thus, He is the Helper (نصير) for you, and He has the right to dispose through this path. Therefore, the Glorified has the right to dispose of your affairs from whichever path you take. In His saying: **وَمَا لَكُم مِّن دُونِ اللَّهِ** (And besides Allah you do not have...), the explicit noun was used in place of the pronoun in view of the sentence being like an independent piece of speech, as the answer is complete without it.

From what has passed, it has become clear: **First**, that abrogation is not restricted to legal rulings but also encompasses existential matters (التكوينيات).

**Second:** That abrogation does not occur without two sides: the abrogator (ناسخ) and the abrogated (منسوخ).

**Third:** That the abrogator contains the perfection (كمال) or interest (مصلحة) that was in the abrogated.

**Fourth:** That the abrogator contradicts the abrogated in its form, and the contradiction between them is only lifted by virtue of both encompassing a shared interest. Thus, when a prophet dies and another prophet is sent in his place—and both are verses among the verses of Allah, the Exalted, one abrogating the other—this is a progression according to what is necessitated by the law of nature (ناموس الطبيعة) regarding life, death, provision, and lifespan, and what is necessitated by the differing interests of the servants according to the difference of eras and the perfection of human individuals. When a religious ruling is abrogated by another religious ruling, all of it encompasses the interest of the religion, and each of the two rulings is more fitting for the interest of the time and more suitable for the state of the believers—such as the ruling of forgiveness at the beginning of the call when the Muslims had neither numbers nor equipment, and the ruling of Jihad after that when Islam became strong, and they prepared what power they could and struck terror into the hearts of the disbelievers and polytheists. Despite this, the abrogated verses are not devoid of hints and allusions toward abrogation, as in His saying, the Exalted: **فَاعْفُوا وَاصْفَحُوا حَتَّىٰ يَأْتِيَ اللَّهُ بِأَمْرِهِ** **So excuse and forbear until Allah issues His edict** (2:109), which was abrogated by the verse of fighting; and His saying, the Exalted: **فَأَمْسِكُوهُنَّ فِي الْبُيُوتِ حَتَّىٰ يَتَوَفَّاهُنَّ الْمَوْتُ أَوْ يَجْعَلَ اللَّهُ لَهُنَّ سَبِيلًا** **then confine them to their houses until death takes them away or Allah opens a way for them** (4:15), which was abrogated by the verse of flogging. Thus, His saying: "until Allah issues His edict" and His saying: "or Allah opens a way for them" are not devoid of an indication that the ruling is temporary and deferred, and that abrogation will follow it.

**Fifth:** That the relationship between the abrogator (الناسخ) and the abrogated (المنسوخ) is not the same as the relationship between the general (العام) and the specific (الخاص), or between the absolute (المطلق) and the qualified (المقيد), or between the ambiguous (المجمل) and the clarified (المبين). For that which lifts the contradiction between the abrogator and the abrogated after it has been established between them according to the linguistic appearance (الظهور اللفظي) is the wisdom (الحكمة) and interest (المصلحة) present between them. This is in contrast to what lifts the contradiction between the general and the specific, the absolute and the qualified, and the ambiguous and the clarified; for that is the strength of the linguistic appearance present in the specific, the qualified, and the clarified, which interprets the general through specification (تخصيص), the absolute through qualification (تقييد), and the ambiguous through clarification (تبيين), as explained in the science of the principles of jurisprudence (أصول الفقه). The same applies to the clear (المحكم) and the metaphorical (المتشابه), as will come regarding His saying: **مِنْهُ آيَاتٌ مُّحْكَمَاتٌ هُنَّ أُمُّ الْكِتَابِ وَأُخَرُ مُتَشَابِهَاتٌ** **In it are verses that are decisive—they are the Mother of the Book—and others are metaphorical** (3:7).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَوْ نُنسِهَا** (or cause it to be forgotten). It is recited with a *damma* on the *nun* and a *kasra* on the *sin*, from "causing to forget" (الإنساء) in the sense of removal from knowledge and memory, and its explanation has already passed. This is an absolute or general statement not restricted to the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم); rather, it does not include him at all due to His saying, the Exalted: **سَنُقْرِئُكَ فَلَا تَنسَىٰ * إِلَّا مَا شَاءَ اللَّهُ** **We shall have you recite [the Quran] and then you will not forget [any of it], except what Allah wills** (87:6-7). This is a Meccan verse, while the verse of abrogation is Medinan; thus, forgetting is not permissible for him after His saying, the Exalted: "then you will not forget." As for its inclusion of the exception in His saying: "except what Allah wills," it is of the same nature as the exception occurring in His saying, the Exalted: **خَالِدِينَ فِيهَا مَا دَامَتِ السَّمَاوَاتُ وَالْأَرْضُ إِلَّا مَا شَاءَ رَبُّكَ عَطَاءً غَيْرَ مَجْذُوذٍ** **to remain in it as long as the heavens and the earth endure, except what your Lord may wish—an endless bounty** (11:108). It was brought to affirm the persistence of Power (القدرة) alongside the act to change the matter. If the exception were intended to describe what actually occurs in external reality, there would be no meaning to the favor (الامتنان) in His saying: "then you will not forget," since every human and animal possessing memory and retention likewise remembers and forgets, and both their remembering and forgetting are from Him, the Exalted, and by His will. The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was already like that before this promised recitation of favor in His saying: "We shall have you recite"—he would remember by the will of Allah and forget by the will of Allah, the Exalted. Thus, the meaning of the exception is nothing but the affirmation of the absolute nature of Power; meaning: We shall have you recite so you shall never forget, yet Allah is still powerful over causing you to forget this. It is also recited as **نَنسَأَهَا** with a *fatha* on the *nun* and the *hamza*, from "postponement" (نسيء) when something is delayed. The meaning according to this would be: whatever verse We abrogate by removing it or We postpone by delaying its manifestation, We bring one better than it or like it. Divine disposal through advancement and postponement in His verses does not necessitate the loss of perfection or interest. The proof that the intent is to explain that divine disposal is always based on perfection and interest is His saying: "better than it or like it," for "betterness" (الخيرية) only exists in the perfection of an existing thing or the interest of a legislated ruling. In that case, an existent would be similar to another in betterness or exceed it in that. So understand.

## (Narrative Discussion)

Narrations from both sides (the Sunnis and the Shi'a) have multiplied from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), the Companions, and from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام) that in the Quran there is the abrogator (ناسخ) and the abrogated (منسوخ).

In the *Tafsir* of al-Nu'mani, from the Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام), after mentioning several verses of the abrogator and the abrogated, he (عليه السلام) said: "And His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَا خَلَقْتُ الْجِنَّ وَالْإِنسَ إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُونِ** (I did not create the jinn and the humans except that they may worship Me) was abrogated by His saying, the Mighty and Majestic: **وَلَا يَزَالُونَ مُخْتَلِفِينَ * إِلَّا مَن رَّحِمَ رَبُّكَ وَلِذَٰلِكَ خَلَقَهُمْ** (and they will not cease to differ, except those on whom your Lord has mercy—and for that He created them), meaning for mercy He created them."

**I say:** In this is an indication of his (عليه السلام) taking abrogation in the verse in a sense broader than the abrogation occurring in legislation (التشريع). The second verse establishes a reality that necessitates the delimitation of the reality established by the first verse. To put it clearly: the first verse establishes a goal (غاية) for creation, which is worship (العبادة), and Allah, the Glorified, is never overcome in the goal He intends in any of His acts. However, the Glorified created them with the possibility of differing, so they will not cease to differ in guidance and misguidance—they will not cease to differ except for those whom divine care has taken hold of and whom the mercy of guidance has encompassed. "And for that He created them," meaning for this mercy He created them. Thus, the second verse establishes a goal for creation, which is the mercy coupled with worship and guidance, and this only exists in some to the exclusion of all. The first verse was establishing worship as a goal for everyone; thus, this worship was made the goal of everyone from the perspective that some are created for the sake of others, and those others for yet others, until it ends with the people of worship—who are the worshippers created for worship. Thus, it is correct that worship is the goal for all, similar to building a garden and planting a tree for its fruit or for its financial benefits. So the second verse abrogates the absolute nature (إطلاق) of the first verse. Also in the *Tafsir* of al-Nu'mani from him (عليه السلام), he said: "And His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِن مِّنكُمْ إِلَّا وَارِدُهَا كَانَ عَلَىٰ رَبِّكَ حَتْمًا مَّقْضِيًّا** (There is none of you but will come to it: a matter that is a determinate decree of your Lord) was abrogated by His saying: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ سَبَقَتْ لَهُم مِّنَّا الْحُسْنَىٰ أُولَـٰئِكَ عَنْهَا مُبْعَدُونَ * لَا يَسْمَعُونَ حَسِيسَهَا وَهُمْ فِي مَا اشْتَهَتْ أَنفُسُهُمْ خَالِدُونَ * لَا يَحْزُنُهُمُ الْفَزَعُ الْأَكْبَرُ** (Indeed those to whom the best [reward] has been foreordained by Us will be kept far from it. They will not hear its sound, and they will remain forever in what their souls desire. The Great Terror will not grieve them)."

**I say:** The two verses are not of the category of the general (العام) and the specific (الخاص) because of His saying, the Exalted: "a matter that is a determinate decree of your Lord," and a determinate decree (القضاء الحتم) is not subject to being lifted nor is its nullification possible. The meaning of this abrogation will appear from what will come, if Allah wills, regarding His saying: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ سَبَقَتْ لَهُم مِّنَّا الْحُسْنَىٰ أُولَٰئِكَ عَنْهَا مُبْعَدُونَ** (21:101).

In the *Tafsir* of al-Ayyashi, from Imam al-Baqir (عليه السلام): "Among abrogation is *al-bada'* (البداء) — *the change in the apparent divine decree due to the manifestation of a new reality known to Allah from eternity* — which is contained in His saying, the Exalted: **يَمْحُوا اللَّهُ مَا يَشَاءُ وَيُثْبِتُ وَعِندَهُ أُمُّ الْكِتَابِ** (Allah effaces and confirms whatever He wishes, and with Him is the Mother of the Book), and the salvation of the people of Jonah."

**I say:** The reason for this is clear.

In some reports from the Imams of the Ahl al-Bayt, he (عليه السلام) counted the death of an Imam and the standing of another Imam in his place as being from abrogation.

**I say:** Its explanation has already passed, and the reports regarding these meanings are numerous and widely transmitted (مستفيضة).

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Abd ibn Humayd, Abu Dawud in his *al-Nasikh*, and Ibn Jarir extracted from Qatadah, who said: "A verse used to abrogate a verse, and the Prophet of Allah would recite the verse and the Surah and whatever Allah willed of the Surah, then it would be raised and Allah would cause His Prophet to forget it. So Allah said, narrating to His Prophet: **مَا نَنسَخْ مِنْ آيَةٍ أَوْ نُنسِهَا نَأْتِ بِخَيْرٍ مِّنْهَا** (Whatever ayah We abrogate or cause to be forgotten, We bring one better than it), he says: in it is lightening, in it is concession, in it is a command, in it is a prohibition."

**I say:** Numerous narrations were also reported therein regarding the meaning of "causing to forget" (الإنساء), and all of them are rejected due to their contradiction with the Book, as passed in the explanation of His saying: **أَوْ نُنسِهَا**.
